title: Christian denomination

infobox.key: [Principal symbol of Christianity](/wiki/Christian_cross "Principal symbol of Christianity")
infobox.value: | * [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") * [Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")  * [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") * [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") * [Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") * [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") * [Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") * [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus")

is a distinct [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") body within [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") that comprises all [church congregations](/wiki/Church_(congregation) "Church (congregation)") of the same kind, identifiable by traits such as a name, particular history, organization, leadership, [theological doctrine](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), worship style and, sometimes, a founder. It is a secular and neutral term, generally used to denote any established Christian church. Unlike a [cult](/wiki/Cult "Cult") or [sect](/wiki/Sect "Sect"), a denomination is usually seen as part of the Christian religious mainstream. Most Christian denominations refer to themselves as *churches*, whereas some newer ones tend to interchangeably use the terms *churches*, *assemblies*, [*fellowships*](/wiki/Koinonia "Koinonia"), etc. Divisions between one group and another are defined by authority and doctrine; issues such as the [nature of Jesus](/wiki/Christology "Christology"), the authority of [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession"), [biblical hermeneutics](/wiki/Biblical_hermeneutics "Biblical hermeneutics"), [theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology"), [ecclesiology](/wiki/Ecclesiology "Ecclesiology"), [eschatology](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology"), and [papal primacy](/wiki/Papal_primacy "Papal primacy") may separate one denomination from another. Groups of denominations—often sharing broadly similar beliefs, practices, and historical ties—are sometimes known as "branches of Christianity". These branches differ in many ways, especially through differences in practices and belief.[[1]](#cite_note-Ellwood1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Swatos1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-BecchioSchadé1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Richey1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Rhodes1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Wootten1-7)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

A **Christian denomination** is a distinct [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") body within [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") that comprises all [church congregations](/wiki/Church_(congregation) "Church (congregation)") of the same kind, identifiable by traits such as a name, particular history, organization, leadership, [theological doctrine](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), worship style and, sometimes, a founder. It is a secular and neutral term, generally used to denote any established Christian church. Unlike a [cult](/wiki/Cult "Cult") or [sect](/wiki/Sect "Sect"), a denomination is usually seen as part of the Christian religious mainstream. Most Christian denominations refer to themselves as *churches*, whereas some newer ones tend to interchangeably use the terms *churches*, *assemblies*, [*fellowships*](/wiki/Koinonia "Koinonia"), etc. Divisions between one group and another are defined by authority and doctrine; issues such as the [nature of Jesus](/wiki/Christology "Christology"), the authority of [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession"), [biblical hermeneutics](/wiki/Biblical_hermeneutics "Biblical hermeneutics"), [theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology"), [ecclesiology](/wiki/Ecclesiology "Ecclesiology"), [eschatology](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology"), and [papal primacy](/wiki/Papal_primacy "Papal primacy") may separate one denomination from another. Groups of denominations—often sharing broadly similar beliefs, practices, and historical ties—are sometimes known as "branches of Christianity". These branches differ in many ways, especially through differences in practices and belief.[[1]](#cite_note-Ellwood1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Swatos1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-BecchioSchadé1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Richey1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Rhodes1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Wootten1-7)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Individual [denominations](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations "List of Christian denominations") vary widely in the degree to which they recognize one another. Several groups say they are the direct and [sole authentic successor](/wiki/One_true_church "One true church") of the church founded by [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") in the [1st century AD](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_1st_century "Christianity in the 1st century"). Others, however, believe in denominationalism, where some or all Christian groups are legitimate churches of the same religion regardless of their distinguishing labels, beliefs, and practices. Because of this concept, some Christian bodies reject the term "denomination" to describe themselves, to avoid implying equivalence with other churches or denominations.

The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), which has over 1.3 billion members or 50.1% of all Christians worldwide,[[8]](#cite_note-pewforum1-8)[[9]](#cite_note-AnnuarioPontificio-9) does not view itself as a denomination, but as the original pre-denominational Church.[[10]](#cite_note-olsen1999-10) The total [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") population has reached around 1.047 billion in 2024, accounting for about 39.8% of all Christians.[[11]](#cite_note-WorldChristian_Database-11)[[8]](#cite_note-pewforum1-8)[[12]](#cite_note-CSGC-2019-12) Sixteenth-century Protestants separated from the Catholic Church as a result of the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), a movement against doctrines and practices which the [Reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformers "Protestant Reformers") perceived to be in violation of the Bible.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Together, Catholicism and Protestantism (with major traditions including [Adventism](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism"), [Anabaptism](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [Baptists](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"), [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Moravianism](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism"), [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren"), [Quakerism](/wiki/Quakerism "Quakerism"), [Reformed](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"), and [Waldensianism](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians")) compose [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity").[[16]](#cite_note-EB2008-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Melton2005-17) Western Christian denominations prevail in [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") (excluding Eastern Europe), [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), with an estimated 230 million adherents,[[19]](#cite_note-Peworthodox-19)[[12]](#cite_note-CSGC-2019-12)[[20]](#cite_note-20) is the second-largest Christian body in the world and also considers itself the original pre-denominational Church. Orthodox Christians, 80% of whom are Eastern Orthodox and 20% Oriental Orthodox, make up about 11.9% of the global Christian population.[[19]](#cite_note-Peworthodox-19) The Eastern Orthodox Church is itself a [communion](/wiki/Koinonia "Koinonia") of fully independent [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly") churches (or "jurisdictions") that recognize each other, for the most part. Similarly, the Catholic Church is a communion of *[sui iuris](/wiki/Sui_iuris "Sui iuris")* churches, including 23 Eastern ones. The Eastern Orthodox Church, the 23 [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), the [Oriental Orthodox communion](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"), the [Ancient Church of the East](/wiki/Ancient_Church_of_the_East "Ancient Church of the East"), and the [Eastern Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Lutheranism "Eastern Lutheranism") constitute [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"). There are certain [Eastern Protestant Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Protestant_Christianity "Eastern Protestant Christianity") that have adopted Protestant theology but have cultural and historical ties with other Eastern Christians. Eastern Christian denominations are represented mostly in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), [North Asia](/wiki/North_Asia "North Asia"), the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), [Northeast Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"), and [India](/wiki/India "India").

Christians have various doctrines about the Church (the body of the faithful that they believe Jesus Christ established) and about how the divine church corresponds to Christian denominations. The Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, Church of the East and Lutheran denominations, each hold that only their own specific organization faithfully represents the [one holy catholic and apostolic Church](/wiki/Four_Marks_of_the_Church "Four Marks of the Church"), to the [exclusion](/wiki/Religious_exclusivism#Christianity "Religious exclusivism") of all others. Certain denominational traditions teach that they were divinely instituted to propagate a certain doctrine or spiritual experience, for example the raising up of [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") by God to propagate [entire sanctification](/wiki/Christian_perfection#Wesleyan_teaching "Christian perfection") (the "second blessing"),[[21]](#cite_note-DaviesGeorge2017-21) or the launch of [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") to bestow a supernatural [empowerment](/wiki/Third_work_of_grace "Third work of grace") evidenced by [speaking in tongues](/wiki/Speaking_in_tongues "Speaking in tongues") on humanity.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism") emerged after the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") and collectively affirms belief in a [Great Apostasy](/wiki/Great_Apostasy "Great Apostasy"), thus promoting a belief in restoring what they see as primitive Christianity.[[23]](#cite_note-Riswold2009-23) It includes [Mormons](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism"), [Irvingians](/wiki/Irvingianism "Irvingianism"), [Christadelphians](/wiki/Christadelphians "Christadelphians"), [Swedenborgians](/wiki/The_New_Church_(Swedenborgian) "The New Church (Swedenborgian)"), [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), among others, although beliefs between these religions differ greatly.[[24]](#cite_note-OCRT2012-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Bloesch2005-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Spinks2017-26)

Generally, members of the various denominations acknowledge each other as Christians, at least to the extent that they have mutually recognized [baptisms](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") and acknowledge historically [orthodox](/wiki/Orthodoxy "Orthodoxy") views including the [divinity of Jesus](/wiki/Christology "Christology") and doctrines of [sin](/wiki/Christian_views_on_sin "Christian views on sin") and [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity"), even though doctrinal and [ecclesiological](/wiki/Ecclesiology "Ecclesiology") obstacles hinder [full communion between churches](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism"). Since the reforms surrounding the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council") of 1962–1965, the Catholic Church has referred to Protestant churches as [ecclesial communities](/wiki/Ecclesial_community "Ecclesial community"), while reserving the term "church" for [apostolic churches](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession"), including the Eastern and Oriental Orthodox Churches, as well as the [Ancient](/wiki/Ancient_Church_of_the_East "Ancient Church of the East") and [Assyrian Churches of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"). But some [non-denominational Christians](/wiki/Non-denominational_Christianity "Non-denominational Christianity") do not follow any particular branch,[[27]](#cite_note-27) though they sometimes are regarded as Protestants.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

See also: [Christian Church § Related concepts](/wiki/Christian_Church#Related_concepts "Christian Church")

Each group uses different terminology to discuss their beliefs. This section will discuss the definitions of several terms used throughout the article, before discussing the beliefs themselves in detail in following sections.

A denomination within Christianity can be defined as a "recognized autonomous branch of the Christian Church"; major synonyms include "religious group, sect, Church," etc.[[Note 1]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) "Church" as a synonym, refers to a "particular Christian organization with its own clergy, buildings, and distinctive doctrines";[[33]](#cite_note-34) "church" can also more broadly be defined as the entire body of Christians, the "[Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church")".

Some traditional and evangelical [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") draw a distinction between membership in the universal church and fellowship within the local church. Becoming a believer in Christ makes one a member of the universal church; one then may join a fellowship of other local believers.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Some [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") groups describe themselves as interdenominational fellowships, partnering with local churches to strengthen evangelical efforts, usually targeting a particular group with specialized needs, such as students or ethnic groups.[[35]](#cite_note-36) A related concept is [denominationalism](/wiki/Denominationalism "Denominationalism"), the belief that some or all Christian groups are legitimate churches of the same religion regardless of their distinguishing labels, beliefs, and practices.[[36]](#cite_note-Jackson-37) (Conversely, "denominationalism" can also refer to "emphasizing of denominational differences to the point of being narrowly exclusive", similar to [sectarianism](/wiki/Sectarianism "Sectarianism").)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

The views of Protestant leaders differ greatly from those of the leaders of the Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church, the two largest single Christian churches. Each church makes mutually exclusive statements for itself to be the direct continuation of the church founded by Jesus Christ, from whom other denominations later broke away.[[10]](#cite_note-olsen1999-10) These churches, and a few others, reject denominationalism. For the purpose of academic study of religion, the main families of Christianity are categorized as a denomination, that is, "an organized body of Christians."[[38]](#cite_note-BallantineRoberts2008-39)

Historically, Catholics would [label](/wiki/Labelling#Labelling_in_argumentation "Labelling") members of certain Christian churches (also certain non-Christian religions) by the names of their founders, either actual or purported. Such supposed founders were referred to as [heresiarchs](/wiki/Heresiarch "Heresiarch"). This was done even when the party thus labeled viewed itself as belonging to the one true church. This allowed the Catholic party to say that the other church was founded by the founder, while the Catholic church was founded by Christ. This was done intentionally in order to "produce the appearance of the fragmentation within Christianity"[[39]](#cite_note-40) – a problem which the Catholic side would then attempt to remedy on its own terms.

Although Catholics reject [branch theory](/wiki/Branch_theory "Branch theory"), [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") and [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") used the "two lungs" concept to relate Catholicism with Eastern Orthodoxy.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Within the [Restorationist](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism") branch of Christianity, denominations include the [Irvingians](/wiki/Catholic_Apostolic_Church "Catholic Apostolic Church"), [Swedenborgians](/wiki/The_New_Church_(Swedenborgian) "The New Church (Swedenborgian)"), [Christadelphians](/wiki/Christadelphians "Christadelphians"), [Latter Day Saints](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement"), [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), [La Luz del Mundo](/wiki/La_Luz_del_Mundo "La Luz del Mundo"), and [Iglesia ni Cristo](/wiki/Iglesia_ni_Cristo "Iglesia ni Cristo").[[45]](#cite_note-LewisMittelstadt2016-46)[[25]](#cite_note-Bloesch2005-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Spinks2017-26)

Christianity has denominational families (or movements) and also has individual denominations (or communions). The difference between a denomination and a denominational family is sometimes unclear to outsiders. Some denominational families can be considered major branches. Groups that are members of a branch, while sharing historical ties and similar doctrines, are not necessarily in [communion](/wiki/Communion_(Christianity) "Communion (Christianity)") with one another.

There were some movements considered heresies by the [early Church](/wiki/History_of_Christianity "History of Christianity") which do not exist today and are not generally referred to as denominations: examples include the [Gnostics](/wiki/Gnosticism "Gnosticism") (who had believed in an [esoteric](/wiki/Western_esotericism "Western esotericism") [dualism](/wiki/Dualistic_cosmology "Dualistic cosmology") called [gnosis](/wiki/Gnosis "Gnosis")), the [Ebionites](/wiki/Ebionite "Ebionite") (who denied the divinity of Jesus), and the [Arians](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") (who subordinated the [Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son") to the [Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father") by denying the [pre-existence of Christ](/wiki/Pre-existence_of_Christ "Pre-existence of Christ"), thus placing [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") as a created being), [Bogomilism](/wiki/Bogomilism "Bogomilism") and the [Bosnian Church](/wiki/Bosnian_Church "Bosnian Church"). The greatest divisions in Christianity today, however, are between the Eastern and Oriental Orthodox, Catholics, and the various denominations formed during and after the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation").[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49) There also exists a number of [non-Trinitarian](/wiki/Non-Trinitarian "Non-Trinitarian") groups.

### Denominationalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Denominationalism")]

Denominationalism is the belief that some or all Christian groups are legitimate churches of the same religion regardless of their distinguishing labels, beliefs, and practices.[[36]](#cite_note-Jackson-37) The idea was first articulated by [Independents](/wiki/Independent_(religion) "Independent (religion)") within the [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") movement. They argued that differences among Christians were inevitable, but that separation based on these differences was not necessarily [schism](/wiki/Schism_(religion) "Schism (religion)"). Christians are obligated to practice their beliefs rather than remain within a church with which they disagree, but they must also recognize their imperfect knowledge and not condemn other Christians as [apostate](/wiki/Apostate "Apostate") over unimportant matters.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Some Christians view denominationalism as a regrettable fact. As of 2011, divisions are becoming less sharp, and there is increasing cooperation between denominations, which is known as [ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism"). Many denominations participate in the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches").[[50]](#cite_note-51)

## Historical schisms and divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Historical schisms and divisions")]

Christianity has not been a monolithic faith since the [first century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_1st_century "Christianity in the 1st century") or [Apostolic Age](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age"), though Christians were largely in communion with each other. Today there exist a large variety of groups that share a common history and tradition within and without mainstream Christianity. Christianity is the largest religion in the world (making up approximately one-third of the population) and the various divisions have commonalities and differences in tradition, [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), [church government](/wiki/Ecclesiology "Ecclesiology"), doctrine, and language.

The largest [schism](/wiki/Schism "Schism") or division in many classification schemes is between the families of [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") and [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"). After these two larger families come distinct branches of Christianity. Most classification schemes list Roman Catholicism, [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), and [Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Orthodoxy#Christianity "Orthodoxy"), with Orthodox Christianity being divided into [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy") and the [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East"). However Roman Catholicism is to be seen as a distinct denomination within Western Christianity.[[51]](#cite_note-McAuliffe2008-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53) Protestantism includes diverse groups such as [Adventists](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism"), [Anabaptists](/wiki/Anabaptists "Anabaptists"), [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") (inclusive of the [Holiness movement](/wiki/Holiness_movement "Holiness movement")), [Moravians](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism"), [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches"),[[51]](#cite_note-McAuliffe2008-52)[[16]](#cite_note-EB2008-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Melton2005-17) and [Unitarians](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") (depending on one's classification scheme) are all a part of the same family but have distinct doctrinal variations within each group—[Lutherans](/wiki/Lutherans "Lutherans") see themselves not to be a part of the rest of what they call "Reformed Protestantism" due to radical differences in sacramental theology and historical approach to the Reformation itself (both Reformed and Lutherans see their reformation in the sixteenth century to be a 'reforming' of the Catholic Church, not a rejection of it entirely). From these come denominations, which in the West, have independence from the others in their doctrine.

The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), due to its [hierarchical](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy") structures, is not said to be made up of denominations, rather, it is a single denomination that include kinds of regional councils and individual congregations and church bodies, which do not officially differ from one another in doctrine.

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

See also: [Proto-orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Proto-orthodox_Christianity "Proto-orthodox Christianity")

The initial differences between the East and West traditions stem from socio-cultural and ethno-linguistic divisions in and between the [Western Roman](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") and [Byzantine empires](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). Since the West (that is, Western Europe) spoke [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as its *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* and the East (Eastern Europe, the Middle East, Asia, and northern Africa) largely used [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic") and [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") to transmit writings, theological developments were difficult to translate from one branch to the other. In the course of [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_council "Ecumenical council") (large gatherings of Christian leaders), some church bodies split from the larger family of Christianity. Many earlier [heretical](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy") groups either died off for lack of followers or suppression by the early [proto-orthodox](/wiki/Proto-orthodox_Christianity "Proto-orthodox Christianity") Church at large (such as [Apollinarians](/wiki/Apollinarianism "Apollinarianism"), [Montanists](/wiki/Montanism "Montanism"), and [Ebionites](/wiki/Ebionites "Ebionites")).

Following the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") in 451, the next large split came with the [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church") and [Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria") churches dividing themselves, with some churches becoming today's [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"). The [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church"), whose representatives were not able to attend the council did not accept new dogmas and now is also seen as an Oriental Orthodox church. In modern times, there have also been moves towards healing this split, with common Christological statements being made between [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") and Syriac Patriarch [Ignatius Zakka I Iwas](/wiki/Ignatius_Zakka_I_Iwas "Ignatius Zakka I Iwas"), as well as between representatives of both Eastern and Oriental Orthodoxy.

There has been a statement that the [Chalcedonian Creed](/wiki/Chalcedonian_Definition "Chalcedonian Definition") restored [Nestorianism](/wiki/Nestorianism "Nestorianism"), however this is refuted by maintaining the following distinctions associated with the *person* of Christ: two hypostases, two natures ([Nestorian](/wiki/Nestorian "Nestorian")); one hypostasis, one nature ([Monophysite](/wiki/Monophysite "Monophysite")); one hypostasis, two natures (Eastern Orthodox/Roman Catholic).[[53]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg/250px-Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg)

The front door of [All Saints' Church](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Church,_Wittenberg "All Saints' Church, Wittenberg") in [Wittenberg](/wiki/Wittenberg "Wittenberg"), Germany, where [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") nailed his *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")* on 31st October 1517, sparking the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation")

In Western Christianity, a handful of geographically isolated movements preceded the spirit of the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). The [Cathars](/wiki/Cathars "Cathars") were a very strong movement in medieval southwestern France, but did not survive into modern times. In northern [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") and southeastern [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Peter Waldo](/wiki/Peter_Waldo "Peter Waldo") founded the [Waldensians](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") in the 12th century. This movement has largely been absorbed by modern-day Protestant groups. In [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), a movement in the early 15th century by [Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus") called the [Hussites](/wiki/Hussite "Hussite") defied Catholic [dogma](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma"), creating the still-extant [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), a major Protestant denomination.

Although the church as a whole did not experience any major divisions for centuries afterward, the Eastern and Western groups drifted until the point where patriarchs from both families [excommunicated](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication") one another in about 1054 in what is known as the [Great Schism](/wiki/East-West_Schism "East-West Schism"). The political and theological reasons for the schism are complex, but one major controversy was the inclusion and acceptance in the West of the [filioque clause](/wiki/Filioque_clause "Filioque clause") into the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed"), which the East viewed as erroneous. Another was the definition of [papal primacy](/wiki/Primacy_of_the_Roman_Pontiff "Primacy of the Roman Pontiff").

Both West and East agreed that the Patriarch of Rome was owed a "[primacy of honour](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")" by the other patriarchs (those of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), [Antioch](/wiki/Antioch "Antioch"), [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") and [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem_in_Christianity "Jerusalem in Christianity")), but the West also contended that this primacy extended to [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), a position rejected by the Eastern patriarchs. Various attempts at dialogue between the two groups would occur, but it was only in the 1960s, under Pope [Paul VI](/wiki/Paul_VI "Paul VI") and [Patriarch Athenagoras](/wiki/Patriarch_Athenagoras "Patriarch Athenagoras"), that significant steps began to be made to mend the relationship between the two.

### Protestant Reformation (16th century)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Protestant Reformation (16th century)")]

Main article: [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation")

The Protestant Reformation began with the posting of [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther")'s *[Ninety-Five Theses](/wiki/The_Ninety-Five_Theses "The Ninety-Five Theses")* in [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony") on October 31, 1517, written as a set of grievances to reform the pre-Reformation Western Church. [Luther's writings](/wiki/Martin_Luther_(resources) "Martin Luther (resources)"), combined with the work of [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") theologian [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli") and French theologian and politician [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") sought to reform existing problems in doctrine and practice. Due to the reactions of ecclesiastical office holders at the time of the reformers, these reformers separated from the Catholic Church, instigating a rift in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity").

In [England](/wiki/England "England"), [Henry VIII of England](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") declared himself to be supreme head of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") with the [Act of Supremacy](/wiki/Act_of_Supremacy "Act of Supremacy") in 1534, founding the Church of England, repressing both Lutheran reformers and those loyal to the pope. [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer") as [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") introduced the Reformation, in a form compromising between the Calvinists and Lutherans.

### Old and Liberal Catholic Churches (19th–20th centuries)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Old and Liberal Catholic Churches (19th–20th centuries)")]

The [Old Catholic Church](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church") split from the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") in the 1870s because of the promulgation of the [dogma](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma") of [papal infallibility](/wiki/Papal_infallibility "Papal infallibility") as promoted by the [First Vatican Council](/wiki/First_Vatican_Council "First Vatican Council") of 1869–1870. The term 'Old Catholic' was first used in 1853 to describe the members of the See of Utrecht that were not under Papal authority. The Old Catholic movement grew in America but has not maintained ties with Utrecht, although talks are under way between independent Old Catholic bishops and Utrecht.

The [Liberal Catholic Church](/wiki/Liberal_Catholic_Church "Liberal Catholic Church") started in 1916 via an Old Catholic [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") in London, bishop Matthew, who consecrated bishop [James Wedgwood](/wiki/J._I._Wedgwood "J. I. Wedgwood") to the Episcopacy. This stream has in its relatively short existence known many splits, which operate worldwide under several names.

### Eastern Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Eastern Christianity")]

In the Eastern world, the largest body of believers in modern times is the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), sometimes imprecisely called "Greek Orthodox" because from the time of Christ through the Byzantine empire, Greek was its common language. However, the term "[Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church")" actually refers to only one portion of the entire Eastern Orthodox Church. The Eastern Orthodox Church believes itself to be the continuation of the original Christian Church established by [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ"), and the [Apostles](/wiki/Twelve_Apostles "Twelve Apostles"). The Orthodox and Catholics have been separated since the 11th century, following the [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism"), with each of them saying they represent the original pre-schism Church.

The Eastern Orthodox consider themselves to be spiritually one body, which is administratively grouped into several [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly") jurisdictions (also commonly referred to as "churches", despite being parts of one Church). They do not recognize any single bishop as universal church leader, but rather each bishop governs only his own [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"). The [Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople") is known as the Ecumenical Patriarch, and holds the title "[first among equals](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")", meaning only that if a great council is called, the patriarch sits as president of the council. He has no more power than any other bishop. Currently, the largest [synod](/wiki/Synod "Synod") with the most members is the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"). Others include the ancient Patriarchates of [Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople"), [Alexandria](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Greek Orthodox Church of Alexandria"), [Antioch](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church_of_Antioch "Greek Orthodox Church of Antioch") and [Jerusalem](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church_of_Jerusalem "Greek Orthodox Church of Jerusalem"), the [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox_Church "Georgian Orthodox Church"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church"), [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") and [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_Orthodox_Church "Bulgarian Orthodox Church") Orthodox churches, and several smaller ones.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Church_of_Saint_John_the_Arab.jpg/250px-Church_of_Saint_John_the_Arab.jpg)](/wiki/File:Church_of_Saint_John_the_Arab.jpg)

A 6th-century Nestorian church, St. John the Arab, in the [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") village of [Geramon](/wiki/Andac "Andac")

The second largest Eastern Christian communion is [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), which is organized in a similar manner, with six national autocephalous groups and two autonomous bodies, although there are greater internal differences than among the Eastern Orthodox (especially in the diversity of [rites](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") being used). The six autocephalous Oriental Orthodox churches are the [Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria") (Egyptian), [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church"), [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_Orthodox_Church "Armenian Orthodox Church"), [Malankara](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church") (Indian), [Ethiopian](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church") and [Eritrean](/wiki/Eritrean_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church") Orthodox churches. In the Aramaic-speaking areas of the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), the Syriac Orthodox Church has long been dominant. Although the region of modern-day [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") and [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") has had a strong body of believers since the infancy of Christianity, these regions only gained autocephaly in 1963 and 1994 respectively. The Oriental Orthodox are distinguished from the Eastern Orthodox by doctrinal differences concerning the union of human and divine natures in the person of Jesus Christ, and the two communions separated as a consequence of the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") in the year 451, although there have been recent moves towards reconciliation. Since these groups are relatively obscure in the West, literature on them has sometimes included the [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East"), which, like the Oriental Orthodox, originated in the 1st century A.D., but has not been in communion with them since before the [Council of Ephesus](/wiki/Council_of_Ephesus "Council of Ephesus") of 431.

Largely [aniconic](/wiki/Aniconic "Aniconic"), the Church of the East represents a third Eastern Christian tradition in its own right. In recent centuries, it has split into three Churches. The largest (since the early 20th century) is the [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad")-based [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church") formed from groups that entered communion with Rome at different times, beginning in 1552. The second-largest is what since 1976[[54]](#cite_note-55) is officially called the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East") and which from 1933 to 2015 was headquartered first in [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") and then in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), but whose present Catholicos-Patriarch, [Gewargis III](/wiki/Gewargis_III "Gewargis III"), elected in 2015, lives in [Erbil](/wiki/Erbil "Erbil"), Iraq. The third is the [Ancient Church of the East](/wiki/Ancient_Church_of_the_East "Ancient Church of the East"), distinct since 1964 and headed by [Addai II Giwargis](/wiki/Addai_II_Giwargis "Addai II Giwargis"), resident in Baghdad.

There are also the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), most of which are counterparts of those listed above, sharing with them the same theological and liturgical traditions, but differing from them in that they recognize the [Bishop of Rome](/wiki/Pope "Pope") as the [universal head of the Church](/wiki/Primacy_of_the_Roman_Pontiff "Primacy of the Roman Pontiff"). They are fully part of the [Catholic communion](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), on the same level juridically as the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"). Most of their members do not describe themselves as "[*Roman* Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_(term) "Roman Catholic (term)")", a term they associate with membership of the Latin Church, and speak of themselves in relation to whichever Church they belong to: [Maronites](/wiki/Maronite_Church "Maronite Church"), [Melkites](/wiki/Melkite_Greek_Catholic_Church "Melkite Greek Catholic Church"), [Ukrainian Catholics](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church"), [Coptic Catholics](/wiki/Coptic_Catholic_Church "Coptic Catholic Church"), [Chaldean Catholics](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church"), etc.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

And finally the smallest Eastern Christian group founded in early 20th century is [Byzantine Rite Lutheranism](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite_Lutheranism "Byzantine Rite Lutheranism") where accept Byzantine Rite as Church's liturgy while retaining their Lutheran traditions like [Ukrainian Lutheran Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Lutheran_Church "Ukrainian Lutheran Church"). It is considered part of [Eastern Protestant](/wiki/Eastern_Protestant_Christianity "Eastern Protestant Christianity") denominational movement.

The [Latin portion](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), along with [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), comprise the three major divisions of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") in the Western world. Catholics do not describe themselves as a denomination but rather as the original Church, from which all other branches broke off in [schism](/wiki/Schism_(religious) "Schism (religious)"). The [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), and [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") churches are generally considered to be Protestant denominations, although strictly speaking, of these three, only the Lutherans took part in the official [Protestation at Speyer](/wiki/Protestation_at_Speyer "Protestation at Speyer") after the decree of the [Second Diet of Speyer](/wiki/Second_Diet_of_Speyer "Second Diet of Speyer") mandated the burning of Luther's works and the end of the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). Anglicanism is generally classified as Protestant,[[16]](#cite_note-EB2008-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Melton2005-17)[[56]](#cite_note-Hanciles2019-57) being originally seen as a *via media*, or middle way between Lutheranism and Reformed Christianity, and since the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement") of the 19th century, some Anglican writers of Anglo-Catholic churchmanship emphasize a more [catholic](/wiki/Catholic_(term) "Catholic (term)") understanding of the church and characterize it as being *both* Protestant and Catholic.[[57]](#cite_note-AEH2003-58) A case is sometimes also made to regard Lutheranism in a similar way, considering the catholic character of its foundational documents (the [Augsburg Confession](/wiki/Augsburg_Confession "Augsburg Confession") and other documents contained in the [Book of Concord](/wiki/Book_of_Concord "Book of Concord")) and its existence prior to the Anglican, Anabaptist, and [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") churches, from which nearly all other Protestant denominations derive.[[58]](#cite_note-Ludwig2016-59)

One central tenet of Catholicism (which is a common point between Catholic, Scandinavian Lutheran, Anglican, Moravian, Orthodox, and some other Churches), is its practice of [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession"). "[Apostle](/wiki/Apostle "Apostle")" means "one who is sent out". Jesus commissioned the first [twelve apostles](/wiki/Twelve_apostles "Twelve apostles"), and they, in turn laid hands on subsequent church leaders to ordain (commission) them for ministry. In this manner, Catholics and Anglicans trace their ordained ministers all the way back to the original Twelve.

Catholics believe that the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") has authority which can be traced directly to the apostle [Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter") whom they hold to be the original head of and first Pope of the [Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church"). There are smaller churches, such as the [Old Catholic Church](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church") which rejected the definition of [Papal Infallibility](/wiki/Papal_Infallibility "Papal Infallibility") at the [First Vatican Council](/wiki/First_Vatican_Council "First Vatican Council"), as well as [Evangelical Catholics](/wiki/Evangelical_Catholic "Evangelical Catholic") and [Anglo-Catholics](/wiki/Anglo-Catholicism "Anglo-Catholicism"), who are Lutherans and Anglicans that believe that Lutheranism and Anglicanism, respectively, are a continuation of historical [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") and who incorporate many Catholic beliefs and practices.[[58]](#cite_note-Ludwig2016-59) The Catholic Church refers to itself simply by the terms *Catholic* and *Catholicism* (which mean universal).

Some Catholics, based on a strict interpretation of *[extra ecclesiam nulla salus](/wiki/Extra_ecclesiam_nulla_salus "Extra ecclesiam nulla salus")* ("Outside the Church, there is no salvation"), reject any notion those outside its communion could be regarded as part of any true Catholic Christian faith. This is called [Feeneyism](/wiki/Feeneyism "Feeneyism"), which is considered a heresy by the Catholic Church, and was rejected by the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council") (1962–1965).[[59]](#cite_note-UnitatisRed-60) Catholicism has a hierarchical structure in which supreme authority for matters of faith and practice are the exclusive domain of the Pope, who sits on the Throne of Peter, and the bishops when acting in union with him.

Each Protestant movement has developed freely, and many have split over theological issues. For instance, a number of movements grew out of spiritual [revivals](/wiki/Christian_revival "Christian revival"), such as [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism"). Doctrinal issues and matters of [conscience](/wiki/Conscience "Conscience") have also divided Protestants. Still others formed out of administrative issues; [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") branched off as its own group of denominations when the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") complicated the movement's ability to ordain ministers (it had begun as a movement within the Church of England). In Methodism's case, it has undergone a number of administrative schisms and mergers with other denominations (especially those associated with the [holiness movement](/wiki/Holiness_movement "Holiness movement") in the 20th century).

The [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") tradition, made up of the [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish"), [Hutterites](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites"), and [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"), rejected the Roman Catholic and Lutheran doctrines of [infant baptism](/wiki/Pedobaptism "Pedobaptism"); this tradition is also noted for its belief in [pacifism](/wiki/Pacifism "Pacifism"). Many Anabaptists do not see themselves as Protestant, but a separate tradition altogether.[[60]](#cite_note-Klaasen2004-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)

Some denominations which arose alongside the Western Christian tradition consider themselves Christian, but neither Catholic nor wholly Protestant, such as the [Religious Society of Friends](/wiki/Religious_Society_of_Friends "Religious Society of Friends") (Quakers). Quakerism began as an evangelical Christian movement in 17th century [England](/wiki/England "England"), eschewing priests and all formal Anglican or [Catholic sacraments](/wiki/Catholic_sacraments "Catholic sacraments") in their worship, including many of those practices that remained among the stridently Protestant [Puritans](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") such as baptism with water. They were known in America for helping with the Underground Railroad, and like the Mennonites, Quakers traditionally refrain from participation in war.

Many churches with roots in [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism") reject being identified as Protestant or even as a denomination at all, as they use only the Bible and not creeds, and model the church after what they feel is the first-century church found in scripture; the [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ") are one example; [African Initiated Churches](/wiki/African_Initiated_Church "African Initiated Church"), like [Kimbanguism](/wiki/Kimbanguism "Kimbanguism"), mostly fall within Protestantism, with varying degrees of syncretism. The measure of mutual acceptance between the denominations and movements varies, but is growing largely due to the [ecumenical movement](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") in the 20th century and overarching Christian bodies such as the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches").

### Christians with Jewish roots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Christians with Jewish roots")]

Main articles: [Hebrew Christian movement](/wiki/Hebrew_Christian_movement "Hebrew Christian movement"), [Jewish Christian](/wiki/Jewish_Christian "Jewish Christian"), [Messianic Judaism](/wiki/Messianic_Judaism "Messianic Judaism"), and [Subbotniks](/wiki/Subbotniks "Subbotniks")

[Messianic Jews](/wiki/Messianic_Jews "Messianic Jews") maintain a Jewish identity while accepting Jesus as the [Messiah](/wiki/Messiah "Messiah") and the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") as authoritative. After the founding of the church, the [disciples of Jesus](/wiki/Jewish_Christian "Jewish Christian") generally retained their ethnic origins while accepting the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") message. The [first church council was called in Jerusalem](/wiki/Council_of_Jerusalem "Council of Jerusalem") to address just this issue, and the deciding opinion was written by [James the Just](/wiki/James,_brother_of_Jesus "James, brother of Jesus"), the first bishop of Jerusalem and a pivotal figure in the Christian movement. The history of Messianic Judaism includes many movements and groups and defies any simple classification scheme.

The 19th century saw at least 250,000 Jews convert to Christianity according to existing records of various societies.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Data from the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") has it that, as of 2013, about 1.6 million adult [American Jews](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") identify themselves as [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians"), most as [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant").[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-pew:_portrait-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66) According to the same data, most of the Jews who identify themselves as some sort of Christian (1.6 million) were raised as Jews or are Jews by ancestry.[[64]](#cite_note-pew:_portrait-65)

## Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Modern history")]

### Unitarianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Unitarianism")]

Further information: [Biblical Unitarianism](/wiki/Biblical_Unitarianism "Biblical Unitarianism")

Within Italy, Poland, Lithuania, Transylvania, Hungary and Romania [Unitarian Churches](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") emerged from the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") in the 16th century.[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68) They adopted the [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") doctrine of [credobaptism](/wiki/Credobaptism "Credobaptism").[[68]](#cite_note-Bochenski2013-69) The [Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania") is an example of such a denomination that arose in this era and is represented in the [Protestant Theological Institute of Cluj](/wiki/Protestant_Theological_Institute_of_Cluj "Protestant Theological Institute of Cluj"). Due to their rejection of the [Athanasian Creed](/wiki/Athanasian_Creed "Athanasian Creed") which contains the doctrine of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"), many [mainstream Christian Churches](/wiki/Nicene_Christianity "Nicene Christianity") do not recognize Unitarians as Christians.[[69]](#cite_note-Cameron1872-70)

### Restorationism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Restorationism")]

#### Second Great Awakening

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Second Great Awakening")]

Main articles: [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening"), [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism"), and [Restoration Movement](/wiki/Restoration_Movement "Restoration Movement")

The Stone–Campbell [Restoration Movement](/wiki/Restoration_Movement "Restoration Movement") began on the American frontier during the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") (1790–1870) of the early 19th century. The movement sought to restore the church and "the unification of all Christians in a single body patterned after the church of the New Testament."[[70]](#cite_note-I_Just_Want_to_Be_a_Christian-71): 54 Members do not identify as [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") but simply as Christian.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-Neither_Catholic,_Protestant_nor_Jew-73)[[73]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Religion_in_the_South-74): 213

The Restoration Movement developed from several independent efforts to return to [apostolic Christianity](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age"), but two groups, which independently developed similar approaches to the Christian faith, were particularly important.[[74]](#cite_note-Redigging_the_Wells-75): 27–32 The first, led by [Barton W. Stone](/wiki/Barton_W._Stone "Barton W. Stone"), began at [Cane Ridge](/wiki/Cane_Ridge "Cane Ridge"), Kentucky and called themselves simply as "[Christians](/wiki/Christians_(Stone_Movement) "Christians (Stone Movement)")". The second began in western Pennsylvania and Virginia (now West Virginia) and was led by [Thomas Campbell](/wiki/Thomas_Campbell_(clergyman) "Thomas Campbell (clergyman)") and his son, [Alexander Campbell](/wiki/Alexander_Campbell_(clergyman) "Alexander Campbell (clergyman)"); they used the name "[Disciples of Christ](/wiki/Disciples_of_Christ_(Campbell_Movement) "Disciples of Christ (Campbell Movement)")". Both groups sought to restore the whole Christian church on the pattern set forth in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), and both believed that [creeds](/wiki/Creed "Creed") kept Christianity divided. In 1832 they joined in fellowship with a handshake.

Among other things, they were united in the belief that [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") is the Christ, the [Son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God "Son of God"); that Christians should celebrate the [Lord's Supper](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") on the [first day of each week](/wiki/Sabbath_in_Christianity "Sabbath in Christianity"); and that [baptism of adult believers](/wiki/Believer%27s_baptism "Believer's baptism") by [immersion in water](/wiki/Immersion_baptism "Immersion baptism") is a necessary condition for [salvation](/wiki/Salvation "Salvation"). Because the founders wanted to abandon all denominational labels, they used the biblical names for the followers of Jesus.[[75]](#cite_note-McAlister_&_Tucker,_1975-76): 27 Both groups promoted a return to the purposes of the [1st-century churches](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_1st_century "Christianity in the 1st century") as described in the New Testament. One historian of the movement has argued that it was primarily a unity movement, with the restoration motif playing a subordinate role.[[76]](#cite_note-Garrett_2002-77): 8

The Restoration Movement has since divided into multiple separate groups. There are three main branches in the US: the [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ"), the [Christian churches and churches of Christ](/wiki/Christian_churches_and_churches_of_Christ "Christian churches and churches of Christ"), and the [Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)](/wiki/Christian_Church_(Disciples_of_Christ) "Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)"). Other U.S.-based groups affiliated with the movement are the [International Churches of Christ](/wiki/International_Churches_of_Christ "International Churches of Christ") and the [International Christian Churches](/wiki/International_Christian_Churches "International Christian Churches"). Non-U.S. groups include the [Churches of Christ in Australia](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ_in_Australia "Churches of Christ in Australia"), the [Evangelical Christian Church in Canada](/wiki/Evangelical_Christian_Church_in_Canada "Evangelical Christian Church in Canada"), the [Churches of Christ in Europe](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ_in_Europe "Churches of Christ in Europe"). The [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren") are a similar though historically unrelated group which originated in the United Kingdom. Some churches, such as [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ") or the [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren") reject formal ties with other churches within the movement.

Other Christian groups originating during the Second Great Awakening including the [Adventist movement](/wiki/Millerites "Millerites"),[[77]](#cite_note-78) the [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"),[[78]](#cite_note-79) and [Christian Science](/wiki/Christian_Science "Christian Science"),[[79]](#cite_note-80) founded within fifty years of one another, all consider themselves to be restorative of primitive Christianity and the early church. Some Baptist churches with [Landmarkist](/wiki/Landmarkism "Landmarkism") views have similar beliefs concerning their connection with primitive Christianity.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

#### Latter Day Saint movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Latter Day Saint movement")]

Main articles: [Latter Day Saint movement](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement") and [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism")

See also: [List of denominations in the Latter Day Saint movement](/wiki/List_of_denominations_in_the_Latter_Day_Saint_movement "List of denominations in the Latter Day Saint movement") and [Mormonism and Nicene Christianity](/wiki/Mormonism_and_Nicene_Christianity "Mormonism and Nicene Christianity")

Most Latter Day Saint denominations are derived from the [Church of Christ (Latter Day Saints)](/wiki/Church_of_Christ_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Church of Christ (Latter Day Saints)") established by [Joseph Smith](/wiki/Joseph_Smith "Joseph Smith") in 1830, which is categorized as a [Restorationist](/wiki/Restorationist "Restorationist") denomination.[[24]](#cite_note-OCRT2012-24) The largest worldwide denomination is [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints"), colloquially referred to as [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism"). Various considerably smaller sects broke from this movement after its relocation to the Rocky Mountains in the mid-1800s. Several of these broke away over the abandonment of practicing [plural marriage](/wiki/Mormonism_and_polygamy "Mormonism and polygamy") after the [1890 Manifesto](/wiki/1890_Manifesto "1890 Manifesto"). Most of the "Prairie Saint" denominations (see below) were established after [Smith's death](/wiki/Death_of_Joseph_Smith "Death of Joseph Smith") by the remnants of the Latter Day Saints who did not go west with [Brigham Young](/wiki/Brigham_Young "Brigham Young"). Many of these opposed some of the 1840s theological developments in favor of 1830s theological understandings and practices. Other denominations are defined by either a belief in Joseph Smith as a [prophet](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet") or acceptance of the [Book of Mormon](/wiki/Book_of_Mormon "Book of Mormon") as [scripture](/wiki/Religious_text "Religious text"). [Mormons](/wiki/Mormons "Mormons") generally consider themselves to be [restorationist](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism"), believing that Smith, as [prophet, seer, and revelator](/wiki/Prophet,_seer,_and_revelator "Prophet, seer, and revelator"), restored the original and true Church of Christ to the earth. Latter Day Saint denominations are regarded by other Christians as being [nontrinitarian](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism"), with them believing in a [Godhead](/wiki/God_in_Mormonism "God in Mormonism") comprising the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit as separate personages united in purpose, regarding traditional definitions of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") as aberrations of true doctrine and emblematic of the [Great Apostasy](/wiki/Great_Apostasy "Great Apostasy")[[81]](#cite_note-82) but they do not accept certain trinitarian definitions in the [post-apostolic creeds](/wiki/List_of_Christian_creeds "List of Christian creeds"), such as the [Athanasian Creed](/wiki/Athanasian_Creed "Athanasian Creed").

### Spiritual Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Spiritual Christianity")]

[Spiritual Christianity](/wiki/Spiritual_Christianity "Spiritual Christianity"), inclusive of the [Molokans](/wiki/Molokan "Molokan"), *Pryguny*, *Dukh-i-zhizniki*, [Doukhobors](/wiki/Doukhobors "Doukhobors"), and numerous tribal faiths, emerged in Russia, each containing a unique tradition.[[82]](#cite_note-Campbell2000-83) The Doukhobors have maintained close association with [Mennonite Anabaptist Christians](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites") and [Quaker Christians](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") due to analogous religious practices; all of these groups are furthermore collectively considered to be [peace churches](/wiki/Peace_churches "Peace churches") due to their belief in [pacifism](/wiki/Christian_pacifism "Christian pacifism").[[83]](#cite_note-FlemingRowan2004-84)[[84]](#cite_note-DyckMartin-85)[[85]](#cite_note-Fahlbusch2008-86)

### Other movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_denomination&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Other movements")]

Protestant denominations have shown a strong tendency towards diversification and fragmentation, giving rise to numerous churches and movements, especially in Anglo-American religious history, where the process is cast in terms of a series of "[Great Awakenings](/wiki/Great_Awakening "Great Awakening")".

The most recent wave of diversification, known as the [Fourth Great Awakening](/wiki/Fourth_Great_Awakening "Fourth Great Awakening") took place during the 1960s to 1980s and resulted in phenomena such as the [Charismatic Movement](/wiki/Charismatic_Movement "Charismatic Movement"), the [Jesus movement](/wiki/Jesus_movement "Jesus movement"), and a number of [parachurch organizations](/wiki/Parachurch_organization "Parachurch organization") based in [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism").

Many independent churches and movements consider themselves to be [non-denominational](/wiki/Nondenominational "Nondenominational"), but may vary greatly in doctrine. Many of these, like the [local churches](/wiki/Local_churches_(affiliation) "Local churches (affiliation)") movement, reflect the core teachings of traditional Christianity. Others however, such as [The Way International](/wiki/The_Way_International "The Way International"), have been denounced as cults by the [Christian anti-cult movement](/wiki/Christian_anti-cult_movement "Christian anti-cult movement"). Further, others may have similar doctrine to mainline churches but incorporate a multi-faith and ecumenical model such as the Interfaith-Ecumenical Church (IEC) that is based entirely in a virtual and international model.

Two movements, which are entirely unrelated in their founding, but share a common element of an additional Messiah (or incarnation of Christ) are the [Unification Church](/wiki/Unification_Church "Unification Church") and the [Rastafari movement](/wiki/Rastafari_movement "Rastafari movement"). These movements fall outside of traditional [taxonomies](/wiki/Taxonomy_(general) "Taxonomy (general)") of Christian groups, though both cite the Christian Bible as a basis for their beliefs.

[Syncretism](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") of Christian beliefs with local and tribal religions is a phenomenon that occurs throughout the world. An example of this is the [Native American Church](/wiki/Native_American_Church "Native American Church"). The ceremonies of this group are strongly tied to the use of [peyote](/wiki/Peyote "Peyote"). (Parallels may be drawn here with the Rastafari [spiritual use](/wiki/Entheogen "Entheogen") of [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)").) While traditions vary from tribe to tribe, they often include a belief in Jesus as a Native American cultural hero, an intercessor for man, or a spiritual guardian; belief in the Bible; and an association of Jesus with peyote.

There are also some Christians that reject organized religion altogether. Some [Christian anarchists](/wiki/Christian_anarchism "Christian anarchism")—often those of a Protestant background—believe that the [original teachings of Jesus](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") were corrupted by Roman statism (compare [Early Christianity](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") and [State church of the Roman Empire](/wiki/State_church_of_the_Roman_Empire "State church of the Roman Empire")), and that earthly authority such as government, or indeed the established Church, do not and should not have power over them. Following "[The Golden Rule](/wiki/Ethic_of_reciprocity "Ethic of reciprocity")", many oppose the use of physical force in any circumstance, and advocate [nonviolence](/wiki/Nonviolence "Nonviolence"). The Russian novelist [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy") wrote *[The Kingdom of God Is Within You](/wiki/The_Kingdom_of_God_Is_Within_You "The Kingdom of God Is Within You")*,[[86]](#cite_note-87) and was a Christian anarchist.
